desc:
The pebbled trail ascending from the ford goes on snaking uphill, apparently
trying to climb to the high ridges that tower imposingly in the further side of
the vale, to the east. The overhung limbs of an occasional lonely tree standing
by the wayside cast thin shadows upon it, but the landscape is mostly wild-grown
and marked by shrubbery. Here a small track branches southwards, rather faded
and overgrown, and dwindles away into the rough lands.
name: Junction in the Path

desc:
The faint trail marked with white pebbles treads untiringly along the endless,
bushy incline that climbs to the high moors. Those bluffs show faces of bare
rock, like outliers of the distant Misty Mountains. And away over them, the very
Hithaeglir reach up defiantly towards the sky, their bulky bodies of stone
crowned in white, snowy peaks shrouded in clouds.
name: Path to the Misty Mountains

desc:
The trail continues to wind uphill, trying to reach the majestic bluffs that
loom apparently unapproachable ahead, as if rooted out of the earth. When the
moon rises on clear nights, a bluish-silvery glint shimmers in the rims of those
high moors. But here the path snakes though deep brakes and prickly heather, and
through fields of high grass, always along the steep slope. Despite its sinuous
course, however, it seems to lead from west to north.
name: Bend in the Path to the Misty Mountains

desc:
The edge of a ridge of hills lies to the south of here, and the soil becomes
rocky and cracked as it rises toward them. A deep gully, so narrow a deer could
almost leap over, blocks your way westward. Its sheer flanks are made of dark
stone, and the glittering of water can be glimpsed at its bottom.
name: Foot of the Broken Hills

desc:
A few withered shrubs manage to sprout out of the rare cracks in the ground
here. To the west a flat extent of bare rock paves a massive promontory, the
last branch of a ridge of hills. A noisy brook, at the bottom of a narrow and
rocky gorge, can be seen below here to the east and south.
name: Broken Hills

desc:
A brook leaves a deep gorge and flows southward in the middle of a widening,
green valley here. The only real sign of life is a small group of beeches, which
manage to find cover under the face of the huge spur of rock bulging from the
hill to the north. Heavy round stones litter the ground, obviously carried by
the water, and grass and weeds grow amongst them.
name: Widening Valley

desc:
The rocky shoulder of the hill to the north comes down in huge steps to the
level ground at the bottom of the dale. A few lofty fir trees rise among the
lush grass, their twigs arching toward the ground, filling the air all around
with their scent. Not far to the east, a brook hurries in its rocky bed, under
the shadow of a huge cliff.
name: Valley Side

desc:
A long, deep-green dale opens to the east, protected from the chill, northerly
wind by a rank of old, crumbling hills. Patches of thick, wet grass carpet the
valley sides, dying them in an uniform, light-green colour that contrasts with
the blurry, deep shades in the trembling leaves of the scattered trees. But at
dawn, when the day breaks and the light slowly broadens, the whole vale shines
in the bright grey hues of dew.
name: Mouth of a Sheltered Valley

desc:
A thick brushwood covers the ground that in this area looks mostly level. The
tangled weeds make progress somewhat harder, but they don't hinder the quite
splendid views of the near surroundings. To the north and west the earth goes
quickly lower towards the river flowing several miles below. A bit closer, a
half-faded path zigzags through the rising fields, leading roughly southwards.
On the east a deep vale ploughs the ground like a huge dike. To the south the
plains stretch, but high grass slowly replaces the shrubs.
name: Overgrown Highlands

desc:
The landscape around the worn path changes slowly as the leagues roll beneath
your feet. To the south, the ground looks more and more fertile, and all sort of
grasses grow exuberantly on the high plains. Conversely, a rocky soil seems to
prevail on the north, covered mostly in heather and shrubbery. Here the trail
takes a wide bend from north to west, always following the line of the Bruinen
down in the lowlands.
name: Bend in a Faded Trail

desc:
This narrow path cuts through the high, wild lands, leading southwards off from
the Bruinen Vale. The trail looks rather faltering, at times clearly laid, at
other times barely distinguishable, and difficult to follow. All in all, it
seems to follow roughly the course of the river that flows calmly several miles
below, on the west.
name: On a Faded Trail

desc:
The landscape rolls on in a myriad of folds, as though a giant among giants had
tried to press the valley walls together, perhaps willing to close it. Small
clumps of trees abound, with leaves fluttering in the wind and lofty trunks
growing closely. But especially there are fields of fresh grass, a thick, wet,
short grass, fibrous and rough to the touch, capable of enduring the harsh
climate of this region.
name: Folded Lands

desc:
A row of small shoulders stretches eastwards, crowning the southern wall of the
Bruinen Vale that lies to the north. Their broken peaks stand out starkly
against the horizon, especially at dusk, when the slanting light wanes and
shadows spread. On the south, another dale cuts deeply through the country, much
narrower but also richer in vegetation.
name: Edge of a Range of Hills

desc:
All around here lies a folded and broken land. No trees grow on these bleak
hills, but shrubs and some ivy grow where cover can be found against the wind.
At this point the hills seems to have been cloven apart, and a small canyon
falls to the north between sheer walls. Southwards the ground falls, in huge
steps, towards a wide valley far below: from there comes the noise of water and
the scent of trees.
name: Broken Hills

desc:
A tiny rivulet trickles among moss-covered stones at the bottom of this narrow
cleft, so deep and dark that the lowest part is never enlightened by the sun. It
comes from the south, where the ground rises steeply, and turns sharply to the
west. Twenty yards of dark, smooth and slightly undercut stone tower on its
sides: there is no way up.
name: Dim-lit Gully

desc:
The ground here sinks well below the level of the surrounding area, forming a
bowl-shaped depression. Water comes from a gully that opens in a low cliff on
its eastern side, and collects in a muddy pool at the bottom. Surprisingly,
grass, weeds and flowers grow bright and tall on the wet and spongy ground. On
all sides the depression is surrounded by muddy, slippery banks.
name: A Green Bog

desc:
A deep and dark fissure begins here, flanked on either side by high walls, and
descends northwards, widening slightly and getting deeper. Still it is possible
to climb up out of the gully, as cracks in the walls provide good hand-holds. A
small rill gushes out of a crack in the rock, and trickles slowly among dark
slippery stones at the bottom.
name: Dim-lit Gully

desc:
The trail bends here, leading you north to the intersection of paths or east,
towards the grey Misty Mountains. To the west, you see a small barn, but you
find no way to enter it from this side. Vast plains extend to the north-east and
to the south you see nothing but wasteland.
name: Trail to the Misty Mountains

desc:
The trail leads straight east, towards the mountains. It seems to have been out
of use for many years, and it is sometimes hard to find it under the thick grass
that covers it in many places. South of here, you can enter the wasteland, a
hostile area with no sign of shelter against foul beasts and bad weather.
name: Trail to the Misty Mountains

desc:
Grass covers the small trail you are standing on. It runs straight from the west
to the east, taking you into the Misty Mountains in the east. You can see it
continuing in a very straight line, something due to the trails former status as
a part of the Great East Road, built by the Numenoreans of Arnor.
name: Trail to the Misty Mountains

desc:
The trail leads past an old ruin of a house, that is set on a small hill to the
south. The trail is running east-west, and can take you into the Hithaeglir, or
back to the Old East Road and Rivendell to the west. Looking north, you see
nothing but vast plains, and there, in the distance, you make out a high hill,
or plateau to the north-west.
name: Trail to the Misty Mountains

desc:
There is not much left of this old house. The walls are still intact, but that
is about it. The old floor seems to be ready to break any second, and you feel
that you'd better leave this place really soon... You can get back to the trail
to the north, or enter the vast wasteland to the east or the west.
name: Small Ruin

desc:
This part of the land has been devastated by some since long forgotten war.
Nothing grows here, and you can hear no animals, nor any sound from human
beings. But, on a small hill to the east, you see the remnants of an old
building.
name: Wasteland

desc:
This is the beginning of the wasteland, stretching south from the trail to the
Misty Mountains, that is north of here. To the west, you see a small, sparse
forest. As you turn yourself east, you see something that seems to be an old
house or ruin.
name: Wasteland

desc:
You are at the border of a sparse forest to the south-west. East, you see
wastelands, through which you see a small trail leading east to the mountains
far away. But... what is that ? You barely make out a small ruin a bit east of
here!
name: Outskirts of the Sparse Forest

desc:
You are standing among some birches - you could even call it a small and very
sparse forest. It continues to the south and to the east. Looking east, you see
the end of the forest not very far away from here. Beyond the forest edge,
wasteland stretches in all directions.
name: Sparse Forest

desc:
This is the southern end of the sparse forest. Huge blocks, apparently thrown
here by something BIG prevents you from moving any further south of here. To the
west, you see some plains, while the forest continues to the east and the north.
name: Sparse Forest

desc:
You are surrounded by grass and nothing but grass. To the east, you see some
trees, and beyond those, the mountains that Morgoth built a long time ago to
prevent the elves from reaching the shores of the Belegaer, and Aman, the Land
of the Valar further to the west.
name: Plains West of the Forest

desc:
The small forest seems to end here, but you can still go back into it to the
west of the north. You hear some birds singing, but, what was that? A sudden
howl from somewhere inside the forest makes you shiver. You'd better take care
if you decide to go further in among the trees. This part of Arda has never been
the same since the foul orcs of Sauron were here during the siege of Rivendell
in the second age of the sun.
name: Sparse Forest

desc:
Brown grass and small, almost dead bushes cover the devastated land around you.
There is not a single bird singing, and you hear nothing but the wind. In many
places the ground is pierced by large rocks thrusting up through the shallow
soil. The wasteland appears to continue on to both the north and east.
name: Wasteland

desc:
You have found the old cellar of the ruin above you. The damp air is heavy to
breathe, and the dark earth walls are dripping with water... A chill runs down
your spine, and you have a very un- pleasant feeling of not being alone down
here. Something certainly is waiting for you somewhere here in the darkness.
name: In the Dark Cellar

desc:
This small, dirty hole is obviously home to some foxes. You wonder how anyone
can freely settle in a simple hole in the ground...
name: A Small Foxhole

desc:
This passage is low, its earthy walls are filled with rocks jutting out and
tearing your clothes and skin. Roots intertwine themselves on the ceiling and
floor, holding up tons of dirt and stone in their unity. Water drops steadily
from all around, and this passage seems to go on forever...
name: Damp Passage

desc:
You are in black, oily water at the bottom of a well, the rope above being too
high above ones head.
name: Inside the Dark Well

desc:
This used to be the small field of the farm east of here. There is no seed left
here, only the coal black ground with charred bits of straw lying on it is left
to testify that this field was all burnt down not very long ago...
name: Burnt Fields

desc:
You are standing at the old stone-well of the farm. It doesn't seem to have been
used for many decades, the brick is withered, the rope hanging down seems to be
rotten and the bucket is missing. As you look down the well, you see nothing but
a pitch black hole extending deep into the ground.
name: At the Withered Well

desc:
This elevated place is naturally protected by a sheer fall to the north, which
allowed the fortifications to be much lower than west of here. Nothing more than
a few overgrown rocks has survived the many years since it was abandoned. To the
south the ground falls away to sheer cliff also: the hill was cut into long ago,
to extract boulders for buildings and walls.
name: On a Lofty Hill

desc:
A few scattered rocks are all that remains here of an old fortification. The
line of boulders marking where the ancient wall once stood follows the ridge to
the west, while eastward it fades into obscurity amongst the hills. A wide
fissure opens in the sloping ground to the south, and the trickling of water can
be heard from within.
name: Old Ruins among the Hills

desc:
This section of the fortifications was built to bar the gap between two hills,
and is still quite tall and well conserved. It stretches east and west from
here, climbing up the slopes. Ivy completely covers the wall, yet it is
impossible to climb down it, and you will have to make a long detour to get to
the other side.
name: Overgrown Ruined Wall

desc:
A small trail comes out of a wide cleft to the west, and travels between the
ridge of hills that rise to the north and a small copse to the south. Grass and
undergrowth cover the landscape and have partly invaded the track, but even
though some fallen boulders hinder your path the trail is quite easy to follow.
name: Lane behind the Walls

desc:
A small group of pines grows here beside a small creek coming from the hills,
the ceaseless noise of the water filling the copse. All around grass and small
bushes cover the landscape, making travel between the pines slightly difficult.
The pale line of a path encircling this place can be seen from here, travelling
along at the feet of the hills.
name: A Copse of Pine Trees

desc:
A small creek flows out of a wide cleft among the hills to the north, and
proceeds southward by the edge of a small copse of pines. On the other side many
mounds of splintered rock stick out of the earth, and although they must have
stood here a great long time no plant yet grows on them.
name: Waste Mounds

desc:
Some low and broken ivy-clad walls stick out of the weeds and high grass, and
large slabs lie scattered everywhere, half buried and eroded. To the north a
stone quarry was dug, like a deep wound, into the flank of an otherwise green
hill. Here the stone was cut and squared, and westwards beside a small brook
mounds of waste still rise. There must once have been forts and barracks here,
but now nothing remains.
name: A Debris-Strewn Field

desc:
The rocky shoulder of the hill was dug away long ago, and boulders hewn out of
it to build the fortifications that run over the hills not far to the north.
Numerous rocks litter the ground, most of them having fallen from the sheer
hillside, but some do not appear quite so natural - they have flat sides and
blunted edges.
name: Abandoned Stone Quarry

desc:
Gushing out from the base of a high hill on the north, the water has worn away
the calcareous rock and a wide fissure has formed. Scattered on the ground here
are several large stones, half buried and broken. Still, looking closer one can
see flat sides and right angles on them - a sign of the hand of man. A rivulet
follows the cleft southward and soon disappears amongst the rocks.
name: A Wide Cleft

desc:
You are standing within four walls - the only remnants of the small farm. The
only ways out seem to be through the two holes remaining from doors to the north
and the west. Looking around, you see nothing of value among the rubble and
charred bits of wood that lie in the used-to-be farm.
name: The Abandoned Farm

desc:
This used to be the small yard in front of the little farm south of here.
Turning west, you see a small, but crumbled well, that surely hasn't been used
for very long. The main building's roof is gone, and wind and rain hasn't
exactly been nice to it. The small barn, east of here, seems to be a bit more
intact though. North of here, a path can take you east into the mountains or
west, back to the civilised elves in Rivendell.
name: Old Farmyard

desc:
This trail was made to serve the fortifications along the walls. It passes
through the level ground south of the hills and makes a sharp turn from west to
south, following the line of ancient fortifications. An overgrown track leaves
the main path to the north and climbs the hills, disappearing behind a steep
bank. It probably leads to the ruins that can be spotted at the top of the
western hill.
name: Lane behind the Walls

desc:
This small path travels a few yards from the overgrown ruins of an old wall
which stretches northward over a series of small humps in the moors. It seems to
be used now only by animals, and bushes and tufts of high grass invade the
trail. The ground slopes gently to the east where a copse of pine trees can be
seen.
name: Lane behind the Walls

desc:
The western edge of a ridge of hills climbs east of here in a sheer wall of
rock, with a few straggly weeds peeking over its edge. The cliff slowly lowers
to the south, where a line of boulders makes a circle at the edge of a vast
level area. In the middle of the plain rise two low, solitary mounds.
name: High Moors

desc:
The ground is littered with boulders fallen from a crumbling wall of dark grey
stone that climbs northward, at least a dozen yards high. Tiny shrubs peek out
of the chinks in the wall, and small animals hide in holes at its base. To the
south the ground slopes gently toward a narrow valley, where heather gives way
to grass and flowers.
name: High Moor

desc:
Low, gentle hillocks lie all around for miles, with thick heather growing on
them. The terrain is covered with their roots, and their branches sway gently in
the breeze. Southwards the land drops down sheer cliffs many yards to a deeply
cut valley; a noisy brook flows down from the east in it, and turning around the
last branch of a ridge of hills passes out of sight.
name: High Moor

desc:
A noisy brook runs swiftly among sheer stony banks, sprinkling and sparkling on
a rocky bed. It flows in from the east and turns southward, where the sides of
the valley draw near, leaving only a small gap between them and the green
meadows that flank the water. Sheltered from the wind and fed by the water, a
few thin beeches rise on a low, grassy foothill. A few large animal tracks can
be seen around here, particularly nearby the bushes to the north.
name: Deep-cut Valley

desc:
A spring bursts forth from a rock wall to the south, and forms a small pool at
its base; the water feeds a small rivulet, which runs west in a sunken valley.
All around the pool, besides short grass and colourful flowers, many boulders
lie: they are the remnants of an ancient wall that still stretches eastward
along the edge of a wide stretch of level terrain.
name: Deep-cut Valley

desc:
Two mounds lie in the middle of a level stretch of terrain, one barren and
covered with dark rocks, the other with grass among the darker ivy and little
flowers growing on its top. All around in this landscape can be seen ancient and
ruined fortifications: all that remain of a line of ramparts and quarters that
used to stand here.
name: Ancient Battlefield

desc:
A long line of boulders, a few feet tall and partly hidden by thick vegetation,
runs up and down the slopes of a ridge of hills from here. It fades in the
distance shattered and broken in many places, bordering a wide stretch of level
terrain a few miles wide to the north. In the middle of the plains two mounds
rise from the heather.
name: An Overgrown Ruined Wall

desc:
A few marred steps and piles of huge black boulders lie in this elevated place.
Nothing else remains of the ancient temple that once stood here, at the corner
of a rocky plateau, overlooking a deep valley to the north and west. Echoes of a
dreary power still fill the place and no plant yet dares to grow among the
ruins.
name: Ruined Temple

desc:
A low natural crack enters the cliff for a few yards, its entrance hidden by
thick vegetation. The cave is obviously inhabited - there are some tufts of fur
and a few piles of animal waste. In a corner lies a pile of bones, some of them
still dark with blood.
name: Wide Crack in the Cliff

desc:
An odd mixture of grasses grows here. Emerald stalks are intermingled with
short, dark green mosses and blades of medium height. The shades of green range
from being so dark that they are almost black to a pale colour that is little
more than white. To the south lies an immense floodplain that one could easily
mistake for a pond after a heavy downpour. An overgrown trail leads east and
west.
name: Mixed Grass

desc:
At first sight, the seemingly endless plains look monotonous. Upon close
examination, however, it becomes evident that they are made up of a variety of
different grasses. Some stand motionless with their panicles thrust into the air
while others wave feather-like in the wind as if celebrating life. An overgrown
trail leads east and west.
name: Mixed Grass

desc:
The ground slopes gradually downward into the beginnings of a small valley. A
blanket of grass covers the ground, with patches of moss and a few trees. The
footing is fairly even, making it quite easy to travel on a mount. Towards the
south shrubs dominate the view, growing in small clumps in the rocky soil. An
overgrown trail leads west.
name: Mixed Grass

desc:
Under the shelter of the jagged rock ceiling this tunnel is shaded from bright
sunlight and the elements. The rock walls seem to have been hewn by sheer force,
with no thought to architecture or planning. The rock itself is a greyish white,
hard and somewhat brittle. The cave seems to lead down and inward while both
north and south the walls do not look quite right. Among the piles of rubbish on
the floor discarded, and in many cases broken, pieces of armour and weaponry can
be seen.
name: Shortcut

desc:
The wall is cut in a roughly circular pattern. A niche in the wall seems to have
been smashed with a single, mighty stroke of a club, but only rubble lies there
now. The walls curve about you, jagged with stones that threaten to scratch skin
and tear at clothing. A single, crudely made table stands in the centre of the
room, covered with junk. The dust on the floor looks more disturbed than usual,
as if something had been covered up.
name: Roughly Cut Cave

desc:
The dark and cold water flows steadily southward through numerous cracks. None
of them are wide enough for you to pass through. Upwards is a dry and rough
cave. The exit to the east is completely filled with cold and dark water.
name: Flooded Storage Room

desc:
The River Bruinen seems to have a parallel underwater stream flowing westwards
along the river's bottom. The water is cold and dark, and nothing but weeds
seems to inhabit this waterway.
name: Under the Rejuvenated Bruinen

desc:
The water of the river is nice and fresh here, but closer to the bottom it grows
dark and murky. At the very bottom, a weak underwater current flows westward
through an opening in the riverbed. Looking upward, you see the riverbank with a
trail passing along it just above the surface of the water.
name: In the Rejuvenated Bruinen

desc:
A chamber obviously designed for resting, this room is filled with mats of
animal hide and bunches of cloth wadded together to form primitive pillows.
Along one wall bones and scraps of meat have been piled to create a mound of
animal waste. At the back of the cavern old weapons and tools have been piled,
although most of them appear unusable.
name: Cluttered Room

desc:
The rough terrain allows only for meagre plant growth. A few bushes and hearty
weeds manage to survive on the moisture they absorb from the rocky soil. The
ground is somewhat higher in elevation than the surroundings. Any rains that
might fall here quickly flow downward into a valley towards the north, or into a
low-lying floodplain to the west.
name: Shrubs

desc:
Unlike the various grasslands surrounding it, this tiny piece of Arda is filled
with a variety of shrubs and bushy plants. No trees are in evidence. In fact,
nothing higher than a man's waist or lower than a horse's knee grows here. One
might expect grass to fill in the places between the shrubs, but that is not the
case in this landscape.
name: Shrubs

desc:
After a heavy downpour the water seems to hover above the surface before being
absorbed into the soil. The ground seems to be saturated with water without
becoming a muddy mess. This may have once been a flood plain for the nearby
river when it overflowed. Now, however, the river has instead cut a path through
the surrounding landscape.
name: Flood Plain

desc:
Unlike the rolling grasslands to the south of here the raised ground is only
covered by a thin layer of grass, growing closely to the surface. Shadowed by
some foothills further to the west, and by the higher ground just to the north,
very little vegetation grows here. The soil is also rocky, with very little
moisture that might allow some mosses to grow.
name: Thin Grass

desc:
The ground is barely covered with grass; great brown patches of bare earth seem
to dominate the view. For some reason no hearty vegetation ever made its way
here. Rocks are visible in the soil, but do not hinder progress. Unlike the
verdant surroundings they are not covered with any moss, and the presence of
sunlight would prevent any lichens from growing here.
name: Thin Grass

desc:
The bright purple stems of heather dominate the view. As this field is open to
the elements, the plant does not grow very high, perhaps just a few inches off
the ground. The bell-shaped flowers of the plant are visible for most of the
year. Towards the east a plain coverage of sparse vegetation is visible, whereas
the view to the south is filled by a bright green sea of grass.
name: Thin Grass

desc:
Supplied by rainwater flowing down from some foothills to the east, and higher
ground to the north, hearty grass thrives here, covering the ground almost
completely. An occasional large rock stands alone against the verdant backdrop,
perhaps the remains of a great boulder that once rolled down from the mountains
of the Misty Mountains far to the east.
name: Grasslands

desc:
Unlike the sparse ground cover to the north of here, the tall sea of grass seems
to go on for miles in all directions, except to the south-west where the land
takes on a marshy appearance. Here, small burrowing animals or an occasional
snake can be seen darting about among the thick blades of grass.
name: Grasslands

desc:
This is the northwestern corner of what seems to be a great grassland area that
continues onwards east and southeast for miles. Although there are a few hills
the entire area seems to be on raised ground, or perhaps it is just that to the
south the marshy ground has sunken deeper. Bright, violet heather seems to grow
along the northern edge of this wading grass.
name: Grasslands

desc:
A bright, green grass covers the entire landscape. Even in the colder months of
the year the blades manage to maintain their appearance, encased in frost or ice
as the weather dictates. In the warm seasons a quiet wind can be seen rippling
across the surface, like waves across a great, gentle ocean; but there is no
ocean nearby, only the River Bruinen flowing far to the west.
name: Grasslands

desc:
This was perhaps a section of foothills of the Misty Mountains farther to the
east. The plain is by no means flat as the name might imply. It rolls forward to
the south, as if the passage of years had eroded the hills and shaped the
landscape to become a series of peaks and valleys that are neither very tall nor
very deep. To the west, behind thich bushes, the sides of these undulating
plains dip downwards into a wet lowland. Thorny bushes prevents you going west.
name: A Hilly Plain

desc:
Perhaps these plains were once part of the floodplains of the mighty Bruinen in
times past, or of some unknown river that flowed from the Misty Mountains, but
now has run dry. One could imagine that the waving plains were a series of
natural levees and that the water would flow over one and into the next,
receding over decades to leave behind this odd plain, the land fertile for plant
life. Thorny bushes prevents you going west.
name: A Hilly Plain

desc:
The ground underfoot is quite rich in nutrients. This is clear from the dark
soil that lies here, almost free of rocks. Large puddles are formed on the
surface after rainstorms, an inviting sight for various birds. Small bushes to
the east seem to be a nesting ground for quail and other game birds, which can
sometimes be seen here.
name: A Hilly Plain

desc:
Because a fen such as this would normally lie close to a river, one could guess
that there is an underground river or other large water source flowing deep
under the ground that surfaces here. The fen seems to continue on for some
distance to the west, making it impossible to ride. Towards the south, one would
have to wade some distance to reach solid ground.
name: Alluvial Fen

desc:
This small hill stands at the edge of a large swamp, almost as if placed there
as a sentinel. It is covered by grass and small plants, and the crown of the
hill is littered by small yellow flowers. Not much can be heard except the
buzzing from the swamps to the west, but the view is excellent. The landscape
all around here is like a large thick blanket of green, billowing in the wind.
On the horizon, far to the southwest, an old tower can be spotted in the haze.
name: A Small Hill

desc:
Standing just north of the stream, this small hill provides it with water in the
early spring as snow melts and the water starts to flow down along the sides of
the hill. The hill itself is rather low and flat, but its location near the
stream makes it thrive, the grass and flowers covering it completely in a
colourful pattern when spring sets in.
name: A Small Hill

desc:
Here the small stream starts its westward passage. Already growing in strength
and speed, it still has a long way to go before it becomes the wild and rapid
river that joins up with Bruinen far to the west of here. Flowing sedately
westwards, it makes its way through the small hills.
name: A Narrow Stream

desc:
Small trickling streams of water, slowly making their way here from the nearby
hills, gather to form the beginning of one of the tributaries to the mighty and
powerful river Bruinen. As the water gathers and begins its arduous journey
west, it is the birth of a small, albeit steady, stream of life.
name: The Birth of the Tributary

desc:
The stream runs swiftly here, between hills to the north and the grassy plains
to the south. Not yet a river, it rushes west as fast as it can, eager to join
Bruinen. West of here it seems to slow down, broadening as it reaches the
marshlands where it joins with other small streams to form a river.
name: A Stream by the Hills

desc:
The small stream to the east becomes a river here, broader and deeper but not
any swifter. Much of the river merges with the surrounding marshland, becoming
stagnant in the swamps to the north. Another small stream flows in from the
swampland, forming a delta. Together they form one of the many tributaries of
the Bruinen.
name: A Marshy Stream

desc:
Hidden in a crevice, this camp is carefully concealed within the side of the
slopes of the hill. Bushes and rocks ensure that the entrance to the small
cavern is not found from the outside. In the back of the crevice what seems to
be small hand and footholds have been carved out in the rock to allow passage up
to a small rocky ledge some meters above. A small metal plaque is attached to
the wall below the ledge.
name: Hidden Camp

desc:
Covered by nondescript grass, this small hill is notably different from the
marshlands north and west. It has no special features or marks, nor does it seem
to be of any interest to the buzzing clouds of stinging insects that inhabit the
swamps.
name: A Hill in the Swamps

desc:
Everything is mud - below you and all around you. You even breathe mud. The air
is filled with bloodsucking insects, making your passage almost a living hell,
filling your mouth as you gasp for breath and stinging you even if you try to
cover your face and hands.
name: A Muddy Swamp

desc:
The fen is host to very few plants or animals above the surface. The occasional
swarm of midges or mosquito frequent the area, but very few animals that are not
of an aquatic nature would be tempted to stay long and even those would likely
prefer the marshes or swamps further south. To the west a large tree is visible
in the distance.
name: Alluvial Fen

desc:
This landscape seems to be both in the midst of transformation and halted in
time. The firm ground of the plains is slowly losing ground to the wet and
marshy ground that borders it on the north and west, and to the wetter fens that
lie to the south. This seems to be one of the few patches of truly firm ground
for a few miles in each direction.
name: Roots of a Lone Tree

desc:
The soft earth is covered by a bright green moss, and at times with sparse grass
or other low-lying vegetation. To the north the marshy ground continues. East is
blocked by dense bushes and south of here the plains seem to rise slightly,
causing the ground to be more solid and able to sustain heartier plant life.
Thorny bushes prevent you going east.
name: Marshy Plains

desc:
The ground here slopes upward toward the north, rising gently up out of the
surrounding marshy ground. At this angle it cannot retain the same amount of
groundwater as the marshes around it, making it somewhat firmer. The ground
mists that are sometimes visible on the lower ground do not occur here, and the
ground itself is covered with short grass. Thorny bushes prevent you going east.
name: Marshy Ground

desc:
When a breeze blows across the surface of the dark green grass it seems to sway
and move in time with some unknown rhythm, showing the pale underside of the
blades. The grass continues on north and east, but to the west lies something
quite different. The ground there is sunken as if some great weight had been
pushing down over time, creating a low-lying marshy area.
name: Grasslands

desc:
The soft footing can be perilous if one were to tread without caution. The area
almost seems to be in a transition from becoming a plain of solid ground and
grass to a loamy surface of moss. To the north and east the ground is higher; it
would be difficult to climb up there from here. There are only a few roots and
twigs on the slopes, and nothing that could secure the ascent.
name: Muddy Ground

desc:
The ground underfoot is a mixture of both a soft, moist soil to the north and
firm earth to the east, where a slope ascends gradually out of the marshes. A
low mist often hangs above the ground at about the height of a man's knee. Some
holes in the ground in various locations are signs that snakes live nearby.
name: Marshy Ground

desc:
Perhaps long ago this was once part of the course of the Bruinen, before it
carved its path out of the landscape far to the northwest, or maybe some mighty
underground river flows nearby. Whatever the reason the ground is quite wet and
very soft underfoot. To the west, the surface is sunken deep and appears quite
waterlogged.
name: Marshy Plains

desc:
The soft ground underfoot is sometimes slippery. A few rocks are visible on the
ground, covered with a bright green moss. A heavy, damp feeling pervades the air
and there are frequent ground mists, making travel difficult at times. There is
also peat, but no evidence that anyone has been using it. Towards the south, a
large tree is clearly visible.
name: Quagmire

desc:
An immense oak tree stands at the centre of the marshy ground, buoyed by its
large root structure. A few small leaves cling to the branches, but the tree is
half-drowned as the lowlands sink even lower into the depths of Arda. To the
west solid ground is visible. On all sides tough marshes and fens seem to be the
only visible landscape. Far off to the east the caps of the Misty Mountains are
barely discernible in the distance.
name: Alluvial Fen

desc:
The air is damp. A wall of branches and leaves block out the sky. But still
there is a certain amount of freedom since you can leave in almost any
direction, climbing out on long branches, let yourself safely drop down to the
ground or just simply climb back the way you came.
name: In a Lone Tree

desc:
You find yourself just south of a small stream, on the edge of a vast ocean of
grass. Here, the undergrowth is thick and healthy, being so close to the water.
To the south the landscape changes colour, becoming bleaker as you look towards
the horizon.
name: Thriving Plains

desc:
The ground is soft here, every footstep sinking down a few inches into the
earth. Just north of here you can hear the soft trickling of a stream running
by, and the sounds of a few small birds above it. As you gaze around you see
that the plains continue in all directions, but that they seem to be more hilly
and perhaps more marshy on the other side of the stream.
name: The Marshy Plains

desc:
Here the ground is covered totally by grass in every direction you look. High
and thick, it makes travel slightly taxing, as movement is little more than
wading through. Just to your west you see something that looks like a dark
streak through the plains. Far to the southeast, the plains give way to hills.
name: Green Plains

desc:
A rather large boulder, over one yard across, lies here, almost completely
overgrown. A few bird droppings on the top of the boulder show it to be a
popular place to watch for prey. Looking around, you can see a hill a bit to the
south of here and, far to the north, the blue ribbon of a river stretching
through the plains.
name: Rock in the Grass

desc:
A soft trickling can be heard from the north, the faraway sound of water
flowing. Even though the noise is steady and firm, it is soft and low. A bird
calling out high up in the sky and the light breathing of the wind create a
peaceful, yet strong and living, feeling in this place. Everything is green as
far as the eye can see, and the only things changing are the waves flowing
towards you as the grasses bend to the winds.
name: Flowing Plains

desc:
You find yourself at the foot of a rather high hill, rising just southwest of
here. Covered in grass, the hillside would not be discernible from the plains if
not for its height, and the hill seems out of place here, rising so high from
the flat grassy ground. Periodically, gusts of wind come rushing from the other
side of the hill, making the grass flow like waves over the sea.
name: Lush Plains

desc:
The vast, flat plains are broken off here by a rather high hill, rising in the
midst of the grass just south of you. The plains stretch out around you,
enveloping you and the hill in a living blanket. You spot a small rabbit hole in
the ground and you hear, clearly, a small bird singing somewhere in the
distance.
name: Plains beside a Hill

desc:
The plains here are dominated by the high hill rising just southeast of you, and
not much else can be seen in this ocean of grass. Faint sounds can be heard from
the vicinity, possibly a bird calling for its mate, but other than that, the
surroundings are rather quiet.
name: The Green Plains

desc:
The hill slopes upward to your west, slowly climbing as if it was waking up
after a long night's sleep. This hill is the only break in an otherwise plain
and flat landscape, rising majestically and overlooking the vast expanse of the
flowing plains.
name: The Sloping Hill

desc:
You find yourself below a high hill in the flowing plains. Rising high above, it
is an obstacle that you can't pass, thus preventing any passage east. You might
be able to climb to the top if you circle it, passing through the green plains
to your north or through the withered, yellow grass you can spot just south of
the hill. Not much can be heard or seen here, except an occasional bird flying
by.
name: Below the Hill

desc:
Here, on top of the hill, your view is excellent. You can see far as there are
no other obstacles close by. Far west you see the outlines of a tower and
several miles north you can see the blue band of a river. All around you the
plains stretch out, covering the land like an immense blanket, with ripples
running across it as it is flowing in the wind.
name: The High Hill

desc:
Well hidden, this small earth chamber is the hideout of a few of Elrond's elven
hunters. The small room is aligned along the western hillside as to let the
inhabitants have a clear view west, towards the tower. The room is very dark and
only the flames of a small campfire keep the shadows at a distance. A few rays
of light sparkle along the eastern wall, barely managing to pass in via the
minimal watching aperture on the western wall.
name: The Elven Scout's Hideout

desc:
The canyon floor forms a natural depression here as it slopes upwards both
northwards and westwards. Rocks and boulders lie strewn all over the rocky and
uneven ground, making passage here both tiresome and dangerous. Rain water has
collected here in the natural basin. Surrounding the small icy pool of water,
small patches of green grow, trying to conquer the harsh, rocky surroundings.
name: Canyon Floor

desc:
Steep walls lead up from the canyon floor, ending in a hazy blur far far above.
The floor is littered with boulders and debris from minor rockslides and slopes
gently southwards where the canyon continues. The walls are covered with cracks
and crevices from which can be heard the soft sounds of scales moving on rock.
name: Canyon Floor

desc:
Like a scar in the rock, the canyon cuts deep into the mountain base here. The
floor is covered with an abundance of rocks in all sizes and shapes. Rising high
above you, the steep walls of the canyon contain numerous crevices and dark and
foreboding cracks. High above, the sky can be seen, almost vanishing as the
rocky walls seem to close in on you.
name: Canyon Floor

desc:
Though the lake is pitch black, because it is shallow the small stones and sand
which cover the bottom can be seen. Along the bottom of the lake there are also
a few larger rocks and even a small boulder or two scattered among the small
stones. The walls are lined with a thin layer of algae and a few small, aquatic
plants grow in the dark, rich sand. To the south the bottom of the lake slopes
upwards, forming a shoreline at the back of the cave.
name: A Dark, Underground Pool

desc:
The roof of the tower has always been used as a lookout. Even now, when orcs
inhabit the tower, they stand guard here, keeping unwanted guests from entering
their new home from the air. Although an important post, it is still considered
a punishment among the orcs, as they have to stand post when the sun is up. The
roof has a splendid view over the surroundings, and has small crenellations that
enable the scouts to hide from any enemies view. Remnants of an old flag post
stand in the middle of the roof, but as the orcs want to keep a low profile,
they have not yet placed a standard of their own there.
name: The Roof of the Tower

desc:
This old chamber in the tower might once have been a study chamber or the
bedchamber of someone of importance to the keep. Now it has been turned into the
most important room of the castle, as the orkish shaman resides here. The cracks
in the walls also enable the orcs to use this room as a lookout post where they
keep watch on their front door.
name: The Cracked Chamber

desc:
This tower is the only remaining part of the old keep, which has fallen into
ruins. The walls are cracked, with small rays of light filtering in through
cracks in the masonry. It does not seem as if the tower could hold back a siege
for more than a short while before the walls would collapse. You see a small
stair running up the eastern wall, and the remains of an old, grand hearth, now
used by the orcs to roast meat, on the northern one. Once the home of some
lord's guard captain, it is now only occupied by the gate guards, who stand
vigilant, watching out for enemies.
name: The Ruined Tower

desc:
The old tower's cellar, once used as a storage for the keep, now holds all the
food and drink the orcs might need. It also contains weapons and armour that
might be useful to hold back an onslaught from the outside. A few orcs also use
this as sleeping grounds, as is evident from the smell of the room.
name: An Old Cellar

desc:
This small passage is about two yards high and about two yards wide. Roughly cut
through the rock below the castle, it was once used as an escape for the lords
inhabiting the castle. These days it is used as a secret entrance for the orcs
that live here now.
name: The Hidden Passage

desc:
The old lord of the castle was a man that had many enemies. In order to feel
more secure, he created this escape passage that would take him out onto the
river, thus to safety, should he ever be attacked. The passage is about two
yards in height and in width, with roughly cut walls. A few torches are fastened
at the walls, but they have long since burnt out. Nowadays the passage is used
by orcs that want to remain concealed, and the walls are covered with dirt and
writings in blood.
name: An Escape Passage

desc:
This small natural cavern, created by the river, is rather quiet considering the
huge amount of water passing it as the river twists and swirls, forcing powerful
streams of water through the mouth of the cave. The walls of the cavern are
smooth, polished by the water, but at the back end of the cavern there are some
cracks and crevices where the water is calm and still, almost peaceful. Just
above the calm waters seems to lie a landing of some sort, with remnants of
wooden pillars used to secure small boats.
name: A Hidden Landing

desc:
Below the bridge the water is always in a permanent state of dusk. The narrow
cleft which the river runs through creates strange echoes and roaring sounds as
the water hurls by. A small cave seems to have been hollowed out by the swift
water just to the south. Not much else can be seen here as you are in constant
movement due to the speed of the water. The walls of the cleft are steep, grey
and slippery, making it impossible to do anything but follow the river.
name: Below the Bridge

desc:
This used to be a small garden in the courtyard, surrounded by the thick walls.
Now the plants have grown savage, and they have managed to ruin the wall. It
still gives some shelter, but mostly it is just piles of rubble. Among the
stones and vegetation sounds can be heard: soft rustling noises, as if something
was being dragged along the stones.
name: Savage Garden

desc:
Once a proud courtyard, vibrating with the busy life of the many men who lived
here and the sounds of horses and men, it is now a deserted pile of rubble, with
very little to discern it from the ruined castle. The castle well remains, but
several large stone blocks have fallen off it and now block the passage into the
tower.
name: An Overgrown Stone Well

desc:
The old yard just inside the castle gates is overgrown with wild plants and evil
thorns. Where once guards in shining armour kept watch now only the wild and
furious vegetation remains, reclaiming its once lost territory. The ruined keep
lies just to the north. Along the northern wall and towering above it like huge
fangs are the remnants of the towers.
name: Overgrown Castle Yard

desc:
Not much remains here that bears resemblance to a stable. A wall has been
knocked down, there is a hole in the roof and several cracks in the remaining
walls. Still, it is dark and it can serve as shelter. It seems as if the
inhabitants left in a hurry, as you can see splinters of several tools littering
the ground.
name: A Ruined Stable

desc:
This small chamber was once the chapel of the keep. Here the inhabitants came
for tranquillity and worship. As the keep fell into ruin, the small chapel has
not been spared. The paintings on the wall have faded and crumbled. What is left
of an altar or a shrine lies in a pile of wood by the western wall. All across
the floor lie pieces of broken stone benches.
name: A Ruined Chapel

desc:
The ledge is small, and even though it is carefully hidden from the trail below,
it is possible to watch any who approach the hidden camp just below you. The
ledge itself is covered with small rocks and boulders, and among the small
cracks and crevices a few plants have gained a weak foothold.
name: Ledge

desc:
The mud sucks at you, trying to drag you down. A short pause to get your breath
and bearings back almost made you a permanent resident of the swamps. Vicious,
buzzing insects fly all around you, trying to get as close as possible. Small
itches start to ache all over your body, even in places you did not think
possible.
name: Treacherous Swamps

desc:
Close to where the river and swamplands meet, a lot of ground is under several
inches of water. The few remaining turfs of grass that still grow above water
seem unstable, yielding unsure footing. Underfoot the ground is not at all firm,
but rather spongy and clinging. It sucks at your feet, trying to drag you down.
Endless clouds of buzzing insects surround you, feasting on your blood.
name: Treacherous Swamp

desc:
Perhaps in some time past this was once part of an immense floodplain of the
Bruinen before its course altered to take it far west of here. The southern end
seems to lead deep into swampland. In fact, this seems like a bridge of land
between the swamps and a large fen of stagnant water to the north.
name: Marshy Plains

desc:
The ground underfoot is unstable, making it difficult for one to stand still in
one place. A thick mud covers the surface, drawing anything heavier than a
feather into it. A variety of mosquitoes and the occasional duck inhabit the
area, but even a duck does not stand still very long in this muck. Towards the
south the water overtakes the ground and a large swamp dominates the view.
name: Marshy Plains

desc:
Waters from the surroundings gather here. Silt and gravel are deposited here by
rainwater that flows down from the foothills to the east, and the fens farther
north. This bog is easily travelled on foot but the going is slow. A set of
hills lies further to the south, where the terrain is more easily travelled.
name: Alluvial Bog

desc:
A ridge of hills stretches eastward for miles, like a series of deep folds in
the land. The ground is often broken, providing shelter for the grass and small
bushes in fissures and hollows, while elsewhere heather prevails. To the south a
deep valley opens suddenly at your feet. Many hundreds of feet deep, it is wide
and open - no mere gorge. Woods cover its flanks, and water runs at the bottom.
name: Broken Hills

desc:
A few scattered trees dot this green meadow, between the brook and the sheer
wall of rock to the north. The cliff, noticeably marked with undulating stripes
of darker stone, shields the valley against the harsh wind of the north. It is
high and impassable here, but to the east it lowers and breaks. No track you can
see crosses this uninhabited country.
name: Valley Side

desc:
This storage basement has somehow survived the destruction of the fort, although
the opened chests and broken weaponry scattered everywhere show that this place
was ransacked long ago. Small cells open in the north wall, although their bars
and rusted locks now lie on the ground, half covered in thick dust and debris.
The last steps of the stairs that once led to the upper rooms peek from under a
pile of stones - that passage is now almost completely obstructed. To the east a
large tunnel descends into darkness.
name: Ancient Storeroom

desc:
A large passage follows the wall westwards and rises steeply, blocking your view
of what lies further on. In the past the passage continued to the east also, but
now it ends abruptly at a wall of fallen rubble. A thick layer of dust rises in
clouds at your passage, showing the time that has passed since this place was
held by the Witch-king. A door leads out of the tunnel to the north, probably
used in ages past to sally against the flank of the enemy.
name: Tunnel under the Wall

desc:
The broken ivy-clad walls and ruined watchtower tells you that here stood a
fort. It was erected on this corner of the hills during the long siege by the
Witch-king, probably to quarter his troops and watch the moors against a sudden
sortie. Nothing blocks your view for many miles over the moors and the path
which runs east-west just north of you. It is difficult to walk through the
ruins, because of the piles of stones that are scattered everywhere, sticking
out of the brush now covering the top of the hill.
name: Ruins of a Fort

desc:
At the bottom of two high walls of stone, the ground is covered by smooth,
many-sized pebbles. A brook goes around a massive rocky promontory to the
north-west, sprinkling and foaming among the stones, and the rushing of the
water fills the gorge with echoing noises.
name: A Gorge

desc:
On the shelves are bottles of various green and brown colours covered with dust.
On quite a few of them the surface is somewhat cleaner where some hand has
revealed the name of the container. On the floor is a lot of trash and some
footprints which do not look very old.
name: Wine Cellar

desc:
This small grave chamber is square and dug out in the rocky ground. To make the
walls stronger, the builders put stone blocks as pillars and clad the ceiling
with thin slabs of rock. A small raised dais in the centre of the room is the
evidence that someone powerful and rich was buried here and the violent scenes
on the wall that have been carved and painted into the rock clearly indicate
this to have been a great warrior.
name: An Old Grave Chamber

desc:
In this small alcove, just north of the main grave, a small altar stands on the
floor. Once sparkling in the light of torches, it is now soiled with blood and
dirt. Crude symbols have been painted onto it and what inscriptions that used to
be seen have been scratched into oblivion.
name: A Small Side Chamber

desc:
Once a beautiful hall, decorated with large paintings and shining swords, it is
now a mere ruin of its former glory. Debris and stone rubble lie haphazardly all
over the floor, making the passage arduous. In the western wall a once proud
arch leads way into a smaller sanctuary of the keep and northward the hall opens
up into a large assembly room.
name: Ruins of a Keep

desc:
This was once the great hall of the keep. A roaring fire, large set tables and a
thick fog of smoke hovering all around were an everyday sight. Now the fire has
burned out, the tables are smashed into splinters and the walls are crumbled and
drafty. What is left of a small stone stair runs along the western wall up to
the second level of the keep.
name: A Great Hall

desc:
What is now a great ruined hall was once several smaller rooms like kitchen and
small sleeping chambers for the servants and guards. As the inner walls fell,
the roof caved in, letting sun and rain play wildly with the interior, bringing
havoc and chaos upon the dwindling keep. Not much here bears the resemblance of
the once so strong keep.
name: Ruined Hall

desc:
This once so proud tower is now cracked and jagged with several holes in the
wall. Parts of the roof have fallen in. Water and wind are now free to play
among the remnants of furniture and a large colony of bats that have taken
residence here.
name: Broken Tower

desc:
This small chamber was once the lord's bedchamber. As such it had a splendid
view over the courtyard and the surrounding fields. Several large pieces of wood
lie scattered across the floor - likely remnants of a few large wooden chests
and a large bed. In the western wall is a cracked and broken wooden door that
blocks the passage west.
name: Ruined Bedchambers

desc:
This small chamber was obviously once a chamber for clothes as you can tell by
the numerous piles of rotting textile. Splinters of wood lie scattered among the
piles completing the chaos.
name: A Ruined Chamber

desc:
Leading up from the basement, this old, winding stair reaches for the second
floor of the ancient tower. The steps are cracked and broken in places and every
step sends vibrations through the rock, sometimes causing small pieces of the
old stonework to tumble down the stairs.
name: The Crumbling Stairs

desc:
As you look around you, you immediately can tell that no one has been in this
small barn for many years. Dust covers everything, and you leave traces behind
you. To the west there is a small farm-yard. You cannot proceed in any other
direction.
name: Old Barn
